questions.questionText: Charcot's triad of ascending cholangitis includes:
questions.optionB: Right upper quadrant pain, fever with rigors, and jaundice
questions.explanation: Charcot's triad (cholangitis): (1) RUQ pain, (2) Fever with rigors/chills, (3) Jaundice. Cause: biliary obstruction (usually CBD stone) + bacterial infection. Reynolds' pentad (severe/suppurative cholangitis): Charcot's triad + hypotension + altered mental status. Treatment: IV antibiotics + urgent biliary drainage (ERCP — first line, or PTC). ERCP: sphincterotomy + stone extraction + stent if needed. Emergency surgery rarely needed with ERCP availability. Reference: Bailey & Love — Cholangitis.

questions.questionText: The investigation of choice for gallstones is:
questions.optionB: Abdominal ultrasonography (USG — non-invasive, detects >95% gallstones, shows wall thickening, pericholecystic fluid)
questions.explanation: USG: investigation of choice for gallstones. Sensitivity >95% for gallbladder stones. Findings: echogenic focus with posterior acoustic shadow, wall thickening (>3mm = cholecystitis), pericholecystic fluid, Murphy's sign (USG-guided). CBD stones: USG less sensitive (~50%) → MRCP (non-invasive) or EUS for suspected choledocholithiasis. ERCP: both diagnostic and therapeutic (but invasive — reserved for therapeutic intervention). Only 10-20% gallstones are radiopaque on X-ray. Reference: Bailey & Love — Gallstone Investigation.

questions.questionText: Courvoisier's law states that in obstructive jaundice with a palpable gallbladder:
questions.optionB: The cause is unlikely to be gallstones (suggests malignant obstruction — periampullary/pancreatic head cancer)
questions.explanation: Courvoisier's law: painless obstructive jaundice + palpable, non-tender, distended gallbladder → cause is NOT gallstones (suggests malignant obstruction). Rationale: gallstones → chronic inflammation → fibrosis → gallbladder contracts (cannot distend). Malignant obstruction (pancreatic head/periampullary tumors) → gradual bile duct obstruction → gallbladder distends (previously normal wall). Pancreatic head cancer: painless progressive jaundice, weight loss, palpable gallbladder, Whipple's procedure (pancreaticoduodenectomy). Reference: Bailey & Love — Courvoisier.

questions.questionText: Whipple's procedure (pancreaticoduodenectomy) is the standard operation for:
questions.optionB: Periampullary and pancreatic head tumors (resects head of pancreas, duodenum, distal CBD, gallbladder, distal stomach)
questions.explanation: Whipple's procedure: resection of pancreatic head + duodenum + distal CBD + gallbladder + distal stomach (or pylorus-preserving — PPPD). Reconstruction: 3 anastomoses — pancreaticojejunostomy (most critical, risk of pancreatic fistula), hepaticojejunostomy, gastrojejunostomy. Indications: pancreatic head cancer, ampullary cancer, distal cholangiocarcinoma, duodenal cancer. High morbidity (~40%), mortality <5% in high-volume centers. Delayed gastric emptying: most common complication. Pancreatic fistula: most feared. Reference: Bailey & Love — Whipple.
